speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] married Amato, tape two. Madam Lucia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We arrived when you were in Auschwitz. You explained to me that when the trains arrived, you were stretching your legs. You were happy to have arrived. But you didn't know where you arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they started separating you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, that you've-- you've told your story so far, could you just tell me again how it happened that they were separating you? You went now with your aunt, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my-- my sister called me. Say, come this way. Because here-- you know, when we were in the train, the Italian used to say, give the children to the old people. Because they knew already what was going to happen. Because these Italians they were with the German working. Because they, you know, they had-- they went together with them, but they didn't like much. But they were so nice. They hear that we spoke it Italian and Spanish. Say, you see, when you arrive there, they already killing people, the old people. So better give the children to them, and then you can survive. Because you're young.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they told you that, were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were afraid. We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We believed them. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you did believe them. So you didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. What to do. They used to arrive in Auschwitz, they used to go back themselves. And they knew all the things. So they used to help people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after when we arrived there, you know, they started dividing the people. The old people on one side, and they-- even the young one, they used to pass. It was like a lottery, you know, to pick up and pass through the other row, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we were there, they took us. And from the morning, we arrived-- it was 2 o'clock in the night already, waiting, waiting. They took us into a room where they had the German there shaving. And they were not the Germans. They were the Jewish doing this to the Jewish people to cut, because they were like servants. They used to work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they dressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were dressed like with the uniform. They were [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call. They had the yellow little thing called [NON-ENGLISH], you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: In charge, you see. And they take us-- they start taking us all the clothes fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take it off? Or, did they tell you to take it off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no. They said, take off everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you understand them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like this, take off, you know. And then we took off. And then they-- we sit in the chair there in the floor, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: With no clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: With no clothes. And they start cutting our hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were all our people with we went in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only girls. And they start taking off the hair. And we cry, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they cut your hair with? Was it the machine or a scissor?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, with the scissor, because they were long, the hair. And then after, they had the-- the machine like this. They used to go and even cutting also. They didn't care, you know. And when we saw ourselves-- terrible. You know, first of all, for two weeks we were in the train. We were pale and sick, you know. And when they took us all the hair, we were crying and shivered, you know. They gave us a piece of a rubbish dress to put on. No [INAUDIBLE], no nothing, only that little dress. And it was 2 o'clock in the night. They say, walk, walk. We walk. We arrive in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was 2 o'clock in the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was-- somebody had a watch, you see? Many people, even they-- they hid money in their mouths. I don't know. They had gold money. They had money. But I didn't have nothing. I had one little watch, because I was working. And when I bought it, they took it when we arrived there, you know, and earrings also. And after, you know, when I had the earrings on, to take off the earring they cut my-- my-- my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ear, you know. Look there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, they couldn't pull it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't take it away. I don't know if they pulled it. Look, here. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just hang on a second. Could you just film that, the damage to her ear? Because they couldn't remove the one earring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They couldn't. And they pull it out. Just see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just turn around a little bit. We would like to film that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hang on a second. All right. They actually cut right across.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was bleeding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German. They were taking everything, you know, if you had gold on top. Even if you had rings, you know. They used to cut with a scissor to take it out in case it didn't want to come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they cry when they hurt you with the ear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I cried. Because the blood was coming out, you see. And when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say something to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. They just took off, and we were crying. We didn't know the language. After a few-- few weeks we were there, we learned [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, [NON-ENGLISH], it was running, you know, all the things [INAUDIBLE]. It was, go out, you know? It was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, let's go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any case, we arrived in that--

words.text: any case, we arrived in that--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that-- 2 o'clock, they told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: 2 o'clock. It was 2 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived there in that barrack, you know. That house where they used to sleep these--

speaker: interviewer
text: Block.

speaker: interviewee
text: Block. Block, you see. And we saw all these ladies there. But we-- we forgot to-- we-- we were already shaved ourselves. So I said, look, these people they are men with the boobs-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Bust.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] with the bust. Said, what is that? And after, we realize ourselves also we had no hair. And in the meantime, we were shivering. We were sitting there. And these people there, they are Polish people and Jewish people. But they used to push us. They said-- they used to hit us. The Jewish people because we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stuck together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not let them sleep, you see? And after one hour or two, we were sitting there already. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. Arouse, arouse. They took us outside there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you slept on the floor you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were sitting in the floor, because no bed that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the floor like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It was not in even the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep on snow or did you sleep--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the floor like? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like bricks there. Bricks, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cement or bricks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bricks. Bricks. I think it was bricks. We were sitting there. And then after they took us outside, and they started counting the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just say something? At that moment in time, did you already have a tattoo on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived there, I told you. And then they took out the hair. And then after--

speaker: interviewer
text: They called you for--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't even sleep. Out, out. And then we were crying. And then they count. See, it was [NON-ENGLISH]. And we sit in the floor there in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that snow, you see. And we sitting, and crying, and crying, and nothing for three, four days. After three days we were there, they start calling us to put the tattoo in my hand, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just interrupt and ask you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually sit for three days on the snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. What I mean is when they took us out from the block--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting outside. And then 5 o'clock, it was again [NON-ENGLISH]. And then you-- you could go to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing for three days. We had nothing. We didn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just sat in the block?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sitting and crying, you know? And perish with no food, you know. They used to give us the plate of soup. And [INAUDIBLE], it was watery, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color was it? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? It was like black. It was not nice soup. It was the-- the skin of the potato, you see. They cut off, and they used to call. And they used to give us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, everybody-- the Polish people that were there so many years, they were hungry. They were eating. So they used to pinch our things. We didn't get that time. For three, four days when we didn't like it also. And everybody was perishing, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And every two weeks, it was the-- the selection again, you know. In any case, we didn't eat all this food. And we're working at night time at when it was 6 o'clock to go in. So they used to give us a piece of bread, very thin only with nothing. So we used to keep it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What color was the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brown or white. You know, they used to give us the piece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to go to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it fresh?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [LAUGHS] I-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember. But was it hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they throw us a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hungry you see. So the first time, we ate it. The next day say, if you keep it for tomorrow morning, then you're not so sick. So we used to put under the bed, you know. In the beds, it was wood. We used to sleep seven, eight people together. Many people they were dirty. They had diarrheas, you see. And it was very terrible myself. I used to wash myself with the ice. With the snow, I used to wash. Many people, they were afraid. And after drinking these soups, they used to have diarrheas. And then after three, four days, they had sores in the mouth. Many of our people died straight away. Many people, they became sick and-- and diarrheas. They didn't was themselves, also. And when we arrived there, my period came. And we had nothing, you know. It was running in the legs for three, four days. You know, when you have your-- your period. And after, it didn't come for one year. We had nothing, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, after, when we were liberated, we had. In any case, when we suffered so much. And we were crying and singing, You know that song? And after [INAUDIBLE] with the other girls they were Jewish, Italian Jewish girls. So we made a song, you know. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] You know? We used to sing these songs and-- Carry on. Carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember all the words, you see. Later on, I sing to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say that--

speaker: interviewee
text: And were crying so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you had your-- you had your periods.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had nothing not protect.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing to protect.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you, just in the-- in the block all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the block, no. We used to-- I told you, after three, four days when they did these numbers, they used to call us 4 o'clock in the morning. [NON-ENGLISH] They used to control yourself. And they used to say, you must go [NON-ENGLISH], to work, you see. The work it was to carry blocks of [NON-ENGLISH] What you call?

speaker: interviewer
text: Bricks.

speaker: interviewee
text: To build more rooms for the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So in the night, 1 o'clock, they said, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is for lunch. And after, you know, you stay there three, four weeks, you were hungry. You used to have that soup. So that soup, it was in one plate. You used to have seven people, one little bit, and then pass the plate to the other one.

speaker: interviewer
text: With no spoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: No spoon, nothing. Only this. You used to suck a little bit and give to the other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to share your plate of soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: To share. To share with the plate. And after the people, they were hungry. They used to pinch our food. They used to pinch our bread in the bed. So after a few weeks-- weeks, we were there, we were crying. And we were cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to come and pinch the bread. We used to pinch the blankets. You see, it was the war with the-- with the prisoners. So we used to take their blankets. We used to sleep in the night, they used to pull up. And it was so cold. So my cousin, [INAUDIBLE], she had two daughters, very pretty, beautiful. One, 14 and 15, beautiful girls. Their husband was already killed. He went straight away to the crematorium. But he was young. He was about 35, 40 maybe. And she was so-- Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amelie and [NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: A surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Godron. Godron, you know. So, [INAUDIBLE]. And then she took a piece of blanket. And she put it in the stomach, because she had diarrhea. And after were two, three weeks we had the selection, they took the two children to the crematorium. And she was crying. She wanted to go. And I say, no Auntie, Aunt Amelie, they are-- they are going to come back. Maybe they went to work. They never came back, you see. And then my sister, Felicie, also she got sick. They took her to the river, to the hospital. We never saw her again. So we were myself and my sister. My sister, she was older than me. And I [INAUDIBLE]. I was afraid. I used to look after herself, because she was sick also, you know. And then I'm going to remain alone. My brother was in the other camp. I never saw again, because they transferred him to the other camps, you know, Buchenwald or-- I don't know where he went, [INAUDIBLE]. But he survived. In any case, I was looking after my-- my sister. So my food, I used to give her. I was so hungry one night, so went to lick the barrels, you know. And the German came. He said, [NON-ENGLISH]. Come, come. So they sit here, too. Now, I don't know. And I was crying. They're going to kill me. They had the scissor. I don't know what they're going to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my sister say, why you did this? Why you did this? I said, because of you. Because I want you to survive, you know. So what they did, my hair they were growing already a little bit. And they cut all my-- again, my hair. And they put me in the snow from 6 to 9 o'clock in the evening. From 6:00 in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning? No. From 6:00 in the night--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 9 o'clock. And then I went to work, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they punished you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the punished me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing the-- after it-- I was doing-- after, I tell you I went to the factories. I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you something before we go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I'm talking about the Auschwitz, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Which month was this that you were in Auschwitz now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, we were in August 16. They took us on the 20 of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived on the 16 of August into--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left from Rhodes Island on the 16th of--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 20th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the 20th of July.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived on the 16th of August--

speaker: interviewer
text: August. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So you were telling me now. Can you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we cry and crying all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression when you saw all these blocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We see. And then they used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. No, you are not Jewish. I say, yes, we're Jewish. We used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. They say, you don't speak Yiddish. You look Italian. Mussolini la macaroni, they used to tell us, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you couldn't communicate with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And the morning, they used to give you coffee, black coffee, you know. Sometimes they used to throw you the coffee. Eat-- the Jewish people. The [NON-ENGLISH], you know. They used to give you--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. They used to give us. They throw it on top of you. So I became friends with one girl. She was from Budapest. And she used to say, sing, sing. So I used to sing to her. She used to give me something to eat, you know. And after when we were so hungry, we used to go to the dirty place to take the peeps, even the potato skin. We used to eat them after, you know, from the hunger. We were so hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: After we saw, we were thin, thin, thin. And every week, we used to have selection. We used to cry. How are we-- we are going to come out from here? We say, eh, we're not going to come. We're not coming. One night, I-- I-- I dream, you know. There were horses running. Oh, I said to my sister. I said, I think we are going to go away from here. She say, come on, where are we going? So I say, I saw a-- a-- a carrier with two horses. They were running so fast. So we arrived there. It was the selection that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in the trains where they're taking out the people, you know, for working. So when we arrived there, the trains they were full. They sent us back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every time you were going back, it was like dying. Because you're afraid to die in the crematorium. So when we used to pass the selection, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. And the Germans they were there with sticks. The men?

speaker: interviewee
text: The men, yes. And we, all the people help like this. And you're young, you know? And you are afraid. And you are ashamed of yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to do like this our face to get red a little bit not to go to the crematorium. They used to say, one here, one here, one here. So when my sister passed, and I was not near her. And then after two, they took another one, and I passed. So when I went out, and I embraced my sister. I said, oh, we are alive the two of us. You see? It was-- and we were crying all the time, you know. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were together all the time. But the time we-- we passed through the selection, they took us to a factory from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you-- how long--

speaker: interviewee
text: After six months-- after six months, we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearly six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in Auschwitz six months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then I saw my friends like Diamante Franco, the sister, and plenty of people. They were already selected to the crematorium, who left them there, even my cousin [INAUDIBLE], So we left them. So in the night, they took us in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute. Before we go far away from Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you remember from the other camps? Did they hurt any of the ladies that were in your block?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were crying in sickness. Like, we were going out in the morning. If they see you-- you were helping somebody, they hit you. Never mind I was sitting in the floor in Auschwitz with two stones and my knees down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she punished me for nothing, to help somebody to-- like you say, they were walking to go to the-- to the-- the morning, you know? When they used to call us, you see. And somebody, if she's sick, to help you-- we mustn't help. We must leave them there to die, you see? And the punished me. They put me, and they hit me here, you see? And I had two stones. And my knees, they were down, you know? On the snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the snow. Yeah. And the water--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this--

speaker: interviewee
text: The water it was like this color brown, you know? The water it was very thin--

speaker: interviewer
text: The drinking water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You couldn't drink water. [NON-ENGLISH] It was snowing. You put it in the mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You used to eat the snow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- when you were in Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many blocks there were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were plenty. Because my block-- we were in one block where all these people that they were dying. So they changed to a-- a nice little block, who was a nice [NON-ENGLISH], you know the [INAUDIBLE] Jewish girl. But she was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: In charge. She was very good helping us, because we didn't know the language. It was very hard, you know. And many people they used to speak French a little bit, Italian. So we used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever try to escape from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not myself. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just did as they told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, what they told you. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day. We [INAUDIBLE] with the numbers. [NON-ENGLISH] I don't know the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just hang on a second? We would like to film the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's A-24444. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just read it again. A--

speaker: interviewer
text: 444. A for Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sorry. When they gave you the tattoo on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it sore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because they did it with a pin, you know? And people-- I put it like this, because somebody told me put it like this, and then you can't see it here. You see, like my cousin, she got it here. Myself, I did like this. And you can't. They didn't go by alphabet, you know, Just you were there in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: You were queuing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Queuing, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that according to the numbers of people that were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did it bleed?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit, you know. And then after, we didn't get-- after of this, we didn't get any more. We didn't get about our hair. But we were [INAUDIBLE] when we are going to come out from this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes we hear the sirens. You know, maybe they were coming to-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sirens, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then we used to say, oh, maybe we'll be lucky. And after, we didn't know nothing there. After we went from Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute. During the time that you were there and you were suffering, and they were making you work and carry the stones--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: We prayed always God to help us to go out from there. But the Ashkenazi people [INAUDIBLE]. They used to-- I don't know where they used to get the little candles, you know? And Friday night, they used to light the candle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were reading in Hebrew. [SOBBING] And we used to cry too much, because we-- we saw them praying, you see. These people, they were all the time in shul. We, in Rhodes-- Rhodes, the young people, we never went much, you know, like here. You see? And then-- So you mean the Polish people were more religious than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: More religious. Yeah. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were praying and crying, you know. And then we used to cry a lot. We used to think about our father, how they did the [NON-ENGLISH] Friday nights, you know, all these things, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join in when they were praying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But by doing that, did you not become friends with the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we became friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes they were nice. Other people they were-- I tell you just now. When we were in Auschwitz, these people they used to work in the kitchen. They used to have sisters and friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they say to pinch the potatoes. [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call them. So they used to put them in-- you know, we used to have in the block, we used to have a little stove. And they used to put them there. A stove?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, it's a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to have there. But they used to put the potatoes to-- to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To cook.

speaker: interviewee
text: To toast them there. So we were hungry. My-- my two legs, they were sticking already from the hunger there was. So I used to say to my friend Victoria, I say, I'm going to pinch that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to-- because if you didn't pinch, you will die. So we pinched the potatoes. We give them one each. And when they came to look for them-- but they were fed still, because they used to work in the kitchen. So we took them. You mean the Polish ladies?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish lady. So we ate. And they said, oh, [NON-ENGLISH]. The Italians, they pinched the potatoes. And [NON-ENGLISH], they used to tell us. [NON-ENGLISH], we used to say to them, you know? And after, we became friends. Because everybody there-- you didn't care if it was your mother or father. If you were hungry, you could kill your father to pinch the-- the bread to eat, you see. They used to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So another time, we were so cold. We saw another lady died. We say, we take the blouse. When we want to take the blouse, inside here in the thing, there were so many [INAUDIBLE], [NON-ENGLISH], you know, all the-- Fleas.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but they were [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Ticks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the ticks. Terrible, you know. Full up, because they didn't wash themselves. Maybe they were dying from the sickness. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you wear those blouses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We left them there like this and people dying. And if you see them walking, it was sticks the legs and diarrhea. All the time, diarrheas, diarrheas. [INAUDIBLE]. And the sores, the mouth, they used to grow sore from the water. You see, that water was [INAUDIBLE]. You couldn't drink that water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you drink the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. I didn't drink. Just I should take snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Snow. You used to eat the snow.

words.text: You used to eat the snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my mouth, you see. And then, from there when we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying that you did pray together with the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with the other prisoners. Yeah. They were French also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew that it was Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every week?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they know everything. We didn't know nothing. They knew and we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it make you more religious to know that you were suffering? Did you think there was a God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'd say always. They used say, no, where is God? Where is God? I used to say, there is God. You'll see. He's going to help us. Many of them who I used to talk to, [INAUDIBLE] they died there also. Shame. You know, these [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you still-- yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The sister-in-law, she said, [NON-ENGLISH], we coming out from here? I say, yes, yes. She died also. She was a very--

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you think that you were going to come out of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like I'm going to survive, you know? Because I was young. I was running, and helping, you know, all these things. I thought I'm going to be all right. So when we came out from Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute. Did you-- when-- you said you stayed there for six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You said you left in July.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived-- no, sorry. When did you say you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: August--

speaker: interviewee
text: So August, September, October, November, December.

speaker: interviewer
text: September, October, November, December. January. You stayed there until January?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like this. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do other work as well during the time that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you in different blocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In that-- the block went in-- 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Block 20.

speaker: interviewee
text: Block 20, I think we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if any of the people that were with you, any of the prisoners that were with you, did they do any experiments with them-- the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, one day my friend--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leah [INAUDIBLE]. She died in-- in Milano.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leah who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leah Levy was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was her sister, Rochelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, she was hungry one day. It was-- this in Auschwitz? Or, it was in the factory, I think you-- Oh, it was in the factory when we went out from-- I tell you after.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Auschwitz itself, you don't remember if there were any laboratories where they did research or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't do-- they didn't harm you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, they. They didn't look after, because we were so terrible, you know. No hair. We had a little scarf, you know, on top, white one. You didn't look so nice.
